evidence_quotes.quote: Payment processors should be more like utilities, and thus should not have any say on what payments they process beyond it not being illegal
the entire business model of gacha games is awful, relying on gambling-adjacent mechanics and FOMO. It should definitely be regulated like gambling.
There is no reason why a game that was purchased should stop functioning only because the company who made it doesn't want to maintain it anymore.
Much like repairability is a must, game owners being able to keep it going via either shouldering the maintenance burden, or developing the necessary adaptations should be encouraged by policy.

items.topic: Interoperability Of Digital Purchases Across Devices

evidence_quotes.quote: Outside of games, digital purchases (such as books, movies, etc) should be much closer to their physical counterparts.
Or, to put it another way: it should be easy and simple to read Kindle Books in another eInk device, and the fact that we can't is a travesty.
Finally, chat interoperability is still a concern.
We are stuck in platforms because of the network effect, not because they are great.
There are already plenty of viable standards for the industry to adopt, and yet we are still stuck in a private company's cage.

Network effects should not be a competitive advantage if we want innovation in this area, nor should it be an advantage if we want to maintain sovereignty over our communications, and specially so as we seek to maintain privacy and security of them.

paragraphs.text: Payment processors should be more like utilities, and thus should not have any say on what payments they process beyond it not being illegal (where stopping such payments should only happen in countries where it would be illegal, not as a world-wide measure). This is because foreign payment processors can be put under pressure to make our transactions harder to perform. Alternatively, we need good EU payment processor alternatives. Likewise, the entire business model of gacha games is awful, relying on gambling-adjacent mechanics and FOMO. It should definitely be regulated like gambling. Still within games, initiatives like Stop Killing Games are sorely needed. There is no reason why a game that was purchased should stop functioning only because the company who made it doesn't want to maintain it anymore. Much like repairability is a must, game owners being able to keep it going via either shouldering the maintenance burden, or developing the necessary adaptations should be encouraged by policy. And this doesn't touch on the cultural value of games and how, with existing practices, they are effectively lost and we are left with but a memory of them. Outside of games, digital purchases (such as books, movies, etc) should be much closer to their physical counterparts. It is fair to say that a distributor cannot be forced to distribute forever, but a seller should likewise not be able to stop someone from using their purchase. In that vein, it is beyond difficult to use Amazon Kindle books outside of their ecosystem. Given their dominant, and dare I say monopolistic position in the market (see Kindle Unlimited and its hold in certain markets, allied with the exclusivity position), they effectively force you to use their devices and apps to enjoy your books. Or, to put it another way: it should be easy and simple to read Kindle Books in another eInk device, and the fact that we can't is a travesty. Finally, I would like to highlight that chat interoperability is still a concern. We are stuck in platforms because of the network effect, not because they are great. There are already plenty of viable standards for the industry to adopt, and yet we are still stuck in a private company's cage. As AI is now forced on us, with often little recourse, this has become much more important. Network effects should not be a competitive advantage if we want innovation in this area, nor should it be an advantage if we want to maintain sovereignty over our communications, and specially so as we seek to maintain privacy and security of them.
